title: EXPLORING AND EVALUATING PERSONALIZED MODELS FOR CODE GENERATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## EXPLORING AND EVALUATING PERSONALIZED MODELS FOR CODE GENERATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Large Transformer models achieved the state-of-the-art status for Natural Language Understanding and are increasingly the baseline architecture for source code generation models. Transformers are usually pre-trained on a large unsupervised corpus, learning token representations and transformations relevant to modeling generally available text, and then fine-tuned on a particular task of interest. While fine-tuning is a tried-and-true method for adapting a model to a new domain, for example question-answering on a given topic or a source code generation model, generalization remains an on-going challenge. Here we explore the ability of various levels of model fine-tuning to improve generalization by personalized fine-tuning. In the context of generating unit tests for Java methods, here we evaluate learning to personalize to a specific project using several methods to personalize transformer models for unit test generation for a specific Java project. We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model’s parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific prefix vector. Each of these techniques offers a different trade-off in total compute cost and prediction performance, which we evaluate by code and task-specific metrics, training time, and total computational operations. We compare these fine-tuning strategies for code generation and discuss the potential generalization and cost benefits of each in deployment scenarios.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

It is well-known that even the best models can fail to generalize properly to new domains, and even to new users of said models. For example, a model trained to answer questions in general may not answer StackOverflow questions as well as the questions in the training domain, or a software developer in an Enterprise environment with private code may have libraries and attribute name which differ from public source code used to train a code synthesis model.

The current dominant paradigm in Natural Language Processing (NLP) modeling is to pre-train a large transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017a) on a large corpus and then fine-tune it on a particular task of interest. For example, a question-answering (Q&A) model is generally first pre-trained on a large corpus of textual data for the specific language ( _e.g.,_ Wikipedia, and news articles in English), then fine-tuned on a task-specific dataset of paired questions and corresponding answers. The pre-training process aims at learning semantic vector representation of the language and words, while the fine-tuning process specializes the model for a specific domain.

Transformer models are also increasingly the baseline architecture used for code generation tasks, such as writing methods from natural language description (Clement et al., 2020; Austin et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021), or generating test cases from the focal method under test (Tufano et al., 2021). Similarly for NLP tasks these models are pre-trained on a large corpus of natural text and publicly available source code and then fine-tuned on a specific code-related task. Further, these models also may not generalize to new domains of interest, and can benefit from task or even user-specific fine-tuning, here called customization or personalization. Customization is particularly relevant for code generation models since it provides several benefits:
- the opportunity to improve data privacy by considering private or sensitive data only during the customization process on the client side;
- the opportunity to reduce deployment cost as customized models can offer better user performance without increasing model size.

Custom models can provide clear benefits to users and model providers. We envision serving tens or hundreds of thousands of custom models, but doing so presents several logistical hurdles, including the costs of training, storing, and loading these models into GPU memory for inference. Worse, memory costs will only be exacerbated when working with ever larger and more powerful models.

For these reasons, we investigate several customization approaches, some of which can dramatically reduce the memory footprint and amortized computational cost introduced by custom models. Specifically, we consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which only optimizes the token embedding representations or the final softmax layer; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector prefix.

In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model improvements on a target project in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics. While there is no unambiguous winner among the customization strategies, each approach can provide specific benefits in particular deployment scenarios. This paper provides insights on these customization strategies, their benefits and drawbacks, as well as providing guidelines and suggestions on which one to use based on the training cost, memory and storage, number of users, and deployment scenarios.

## 2 APPROACH

This section describes the proposed customization approach for code generation models. We begin by formally defining the customization process, then we provide details for each of the fine-tuning strategies.

## 2.1 CUSTOMIZATION PROCESS

We use the term _customization_ to refer to the process of fine-tuning a model _m_ , previously trained on a generic dataset for a task _t_ , with the goal of improving its performance on a specific dataset _p_ . The performance of a machine learning model _m_ on a dataset _p_ is measured by one or more evaluation functions _f_ ( _m, p_ ), where _f_ can be either a maximization ( _e.g.,_ BLEU, top-k accuracy) or minimization ( _e.g.,_ perplexity) function. The customization process is designed to modify the trainable parameters of the model _m_ , obtaining the model _m[′]_ , such that the performance of _m[′]_ on _p_ is better than what was attained by _m_ . Specifically, _f_ ( _m[′] , p_ ) _> f_ ( _m, p_ ) for maximization functions, or _f_ ( _m[′] , p_ ) _< f_ ( _m, p_ ) for minimization functions.
In this work, _m_ is an encoder-decoder transformer model, _t_ is a code generation task, and _p_ is a target software project to which we intend to customize _m_ .

## 2.2 CUSTOM FINE-TUNING

Custom fine-tuning is the most straightforward customization approach. The model to be customized is taken as is and trained on a selected project. All parameters are trainable during this process. Figure 1a shows the model during fine-tuning, where all the parameters from the encoder and decoder blocks, as well as embeddings and output layers can be modified.

## 2.3 LIGHTWEIGHT FINE-TUNING - EMBEDDINGS AND OUTPUT LAYER (L-EO)

Fully fine-tuning a model for every project or user may be prohibitive in terms of storage and memory costs. As a result, we explore ways to mitigate these costs by reducing the number of parameters that vary from one custom model to another. In our lightweight fine-tuning experiments, we achieve this

Figure 1b shows the Lightweight fine-tuning - Embeddings and Output Layer (L-EO) design, where most of the model parameters are frozen (displayed in gray), and we allow only the embedding and output layers parameters to be fine-tuned, following the approach in Lu et al. (2021).

## 2.4 LIGHTWEIGHT FINE-TUNING - LAST DECODER BLOCK (L-LDB)

In this lightweight fine-tuning strategy, shown in Figure 1c (L-LDB), most of the model’s parameters are kept frozen, while only the parameters in the last decoder block are trainable, this includes: self-attention, encoder-decoder attention, layernorm and feedforward layers. This design decision of training only the last decoder block is motivated by experimental results analyzing the model’s parameter changes during custom fine-tuning. Figure 2 reports the average absolute changes, during fine-tuning, in the parameters belonging to different Encoder and Decoder blocks for a BART model. We observe that, as we go through the transformer model, the average change in parameter values tends to increase, with the last decoder block showing the highest changes in parameter values. As a result, we hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block and obtain performance improvements similar to the fully custom fine-tuned model.

## 2.5 PREFIX TUNING

Prefix tuning was first introduced by Li and Liang (2021), with the goal of fine-tuning a general model for different tasks. The technique concatenates a sequence (prefix) of virtual tokens (trainable parameters) to the front of the input of every encoder and decoder block. In our context, the intuition behind this approach is that the prefix embeds the properties of a specific project, which allows the model to generate customized responses for that repository. Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 _×_ 200 _×_ 24 _×_ 2 _≈_ 10M trainable parameters. The prefix is initialized to the most frequent words in the repository for which the model is customized.

## 3 EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN

The goal of our experimental design is to investigate whether custom models outperform the baseline model, leading to performance improvements in terms of intrinsic metrics (RQ1), as well as extrinsic task-specific metrics (RQ2). Next, we analyze and compare the different customization approaches in terms of training and compute costs (RQ3) as well as model size and required storage for deployment.

In our case study, we chose Unit Test Case generation as our code generation task _t_ , and _AthenaTest_ by Tufano et al. (2021) as our baseline model _m_ , which is a BART transformer model pre-trained on source code and English, and fine-tuned on Java unit test generation. The task is modeled as a translation task, where the input is a focal method ( _i.e.,_ method under test), and the output is a test case which tests the focal method’s correctness. We randomly sample 20 projects from the test set, each of those representing the dataset _p_ on which a custom model is fine-tuned. Specifically, for each project _p_ , we start from the baseline model _m_ and fine-tune four different custom models according to the four proposed fine-tuning strategies. For each project and fine-tuning strategy ( _e.g.,_ L-EO), we fine-tune and evaluate the models using 4-fold cross-validation. The models are trained until the best validation loss is reached, independently for every fold, every repository, and every customization approach. In total, we fine-tune and evaluate 20( _projects_ ) _×_ 4( _approaches_ ) _×_ 4( _folds_ ) = 320 models.

## 3.1 DATASET

Table 2 reports information about the 20 GitHub repositories sampled from the test set, which will be used to customize our models. The table shows (i) the Project ID, which will be used in the paper to

The list of projects represent a diverse set of repositories with different size, domain, and popularity. They span from small personal projects ( _e.g.,_ Tutorials with 6 stars), to open source projects developed by large organizations such as Apache and Google.

## 3.2 RQ1: INTRINSIC EVALUATION METRICS

**RQ** 1 **: Do custom models obtain better performances on intrinsic metrics, such as BLEU and perplexity, w.r.t. the baseline?** To begin, we investigate how the different model customization approaches described in Sec. 2 score on intrinsic metrics such as BLEU and perplexity. All approaches entail fine-tuning the baseline model to the dataset of a specific project, with the choice of parameters being tuned depending on the approach taken. The four variants are trained independently until the best validation loss is achieved. We report the BLEU4 score and the mean perplexity per token on the test fold, for all the 20 projects. Next, we perform statistical tests to investigate whether the observed differences between the baseline and custom models are significant, as well as differences among the customization approaches. Specifically, we rely on the Kruskal-Wallis test, a non-parametric statistical test.

- 3.3 RQ2: TASK-SPECIFIC PERFORMANCES

**RQ** 2 **: Do custom models improve on performance metrics specific to unit test generation?** We want to investigate how the different customization approaches compare with respect to the downstream task of generating unit tests. Beyond BLEU score and perplexity, we would like to see if custom models can produce the correct target code, how closely their unit tests mimic the repository style, or even if they can perfectly match the desired output.

- _Perfect Matches_ : We compare the model’s output string with the target developer-written unit test. If the two strings are identical, this is considered a perfect match. We do not take spacing and indentation into account as we are using a Java dataset (where indentation is not required). We report the proportion of perfect matches among the top 5 model predictions.

- _Abstracted Code Matches_ : We pass the model output and target output through the src2abs tool (Tufano, 2018), to obtain an abstracted version, masking variable names, method names, etc. We also do not distinguish between different objects of the same type.

- _Coding Style_ : For each project’s custom model, we would like to determine how closely the model learns the developer’s personal programming style and preferences. To this end, we extract the collection of all identifiers ( _i.e.,_ variables and functions’ names) from the unit tests written by the developer as well as those generated by the models. We then pass these text outputs through a tf-idf vectorizer and compute the cosine similarity between them. This allows us to compare the developer’s and the models’ word usage. We examine the similarity between the developer’s unit tests and the baseline and custom models generated tests. This scores the vocabulary similarity of the unit tests with the model generated code.

- 3.4 RQ3: TRAINING COST COMPARISON

**RQ** 3 **: Given the same amount of compute, which custom models achieve the biggest performance improvement?** Since our four training regimes tune a different number of parameters, simply comparing the training time or number of optimization steps to reach the best validation loss may not be appropriate. For a model with _N_ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be _C ≈_ 2 _N_ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers. The backward pass takes roughly twice the amount of compute, but it is unnecessary for layers that are frozen. For additional details, we refer to Table 1 in Kaplan et al. (2020). We report the resulting compute in petaFLOPS-seconds.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 RQ1: INTRINSIC EVALUATION METRICS

Table 3 presents the results of custom models in terms of the intrinsic metrics: BLEU and perplexity. Specifically, for each project, we report the average BLEU and perplexity over the four folds, achieved on the test set by the different customization strategy, as well as the baseline model. We observe notable improvements in both metrics for every project w.r.t. the baseline, with BLEU going from 16.1 achieved by the baseline model to 36-38 by custom models.

The statistical tests reported in Table 4 confirm that the improvement observed by the four customization techniques are statistically significant w.r.t. the baseline ( _p <_ 1e-7). However, we do not observe statistical significance in the differences among the customization strategies, meaning that, in terms of intrinsic metrics performances, the differences are within margin of error.

## 4.2 RQ2: TASK-SPECIFIC PERFORMANCES

The results in terms of task-specific performances are presented in Figure 3. The plot 3a shows the top-k accuracy for perfect matches (solid line) and abstracted matches (dotted line), aggregated over the 20 projects. The baseline model outputs the same code structure (abstracted) in roughly 3% of all cases, and virtually never produces the exact target output (<1%). Moreover, its performance does not improve as we consider more predictions. All customization processes show significant improvement compared to the baseline. Specifically, these improvements are observed for every single project (full results will be available on our online appendix). Customized models produce the correct code structure as their top prediction in _∼_ 13-14% of instances, and a perfect match in _∼_ 4-6% of cases. They also tend to improve as we consider their top 5 predictions. Between the different customization processes, Custom consistently performs the best, closely followed by Prefix and L-LDB. When considering abstracted code matches, these three approaches are nearly identical. L-EO, however, performs slightly worse than the others.

Plot 3b shows the distribution of tf-idf cosine similarity computed between identifiers used in the developers’ written tests and the models’ generated outputs. We observe that the distribution for custom models is skewed towards the higher values of cosine similarity. This result demonstrates that custom models tend to use variable and function names that are more similar to what developers used in their own tests.

## 4.3 RQ3: TRAINING COST COMPARISON

For each customization process, we plot validation loss as a function of compute, as defined in section 3.4. The results are presented in Figure 4, where the light lines represent the validation loss curve for each individual project and fold, while the bold line represents the average for each custom strategy. First note that Custom achieves very large gains during the first epoch, as evidenced by the fact that its validation loss starts much lower than L-EO and L-LDB. Custom also outperforms other customization processes when given a limited amount of compute. However, we observe that beyond a certain amount of compute, Custom and L-LDB tend to achieve similar performances. In contrast, L-EO starts at the same validation loss as L-LDB but converges much slower to the best loss, requiring 2-3 times as much compute.

Since the prefix parameters suffer from poor initialization, Prefix is the most expensive customization process. To overcome this problem, it is possible to first train the prefix on a large generic dataset. Then, given proper hyperparameter tuning, it is possible to substantially cut down compute cost for customizing the prefix.

## 5 DISCUSSION & LESSONS LEARNED

The four customization strategies considered in this work are effective in improving a code generation model’s performances on a given software project. Specifically, all custom models significantly outperform the baseline in terms of intrinsic metrics ( _i.e.,_ BLEU and perplexity) as well as taskspecific metrics ( _i.e.,_ abstract and raw matches). While the differences among the customization approaches are not significant (no clear winner), each strategy offers specific advantages in different circumstances and deployment scenarios.

_**Custom**_ fine-tuning achieves the overall best performances and the customization process is relatively fast and efficient. This is somewhat expected, since this customization strategy allows all the model’s parameters to be tuned on the specific project. This characteristic also leads to the major disadvantage of this approach: each custom model is an entire copy of the original model. Storage and inference costs could become prohibitive when serving many users with personalized custom models.

_**Lightweight**_ fine-tuning achieves good results while training fewer parameters. This allows to serve potentially many users with custom models which can be stored and loaded efficiently. Specifically, L-LDB trains fewer parameters than L-EO, however the latter could allow to deploy the embedding and output layers on the user side, with a privacy-preserving focus.

_**Prefix**_ fine-tuning trains the lowest number of parameters (only 2.4% for a BART model), while improving over the baseline. However, it increases the total number of parameters of the model (prefixes are additional virtual tokens) and requires more compute time to achieve good performances, mostly due to the prefix initialization problem. On the bright side, this strategy allows to batch together requests from different users (with different prefixes), which can be processed by a single model, generating personalized outputs.

## 6 RELATED WORK

This work is related to two areas of the existing literature: neural source code generation and model personalization. Neural code generation has generated an intense recent interest in NLP, using Transformer models Vaswani et al. (2017b) in particular for code completion Svyatkovskiy et al. (2020; 2019); Clement et al. (2020); Raychev et al. (2014); Bruch et al. (2009); Brockschmidt et al. (2018), code synthesis from examples Chen et al. (2018), natural language to code Clement et al. (2020); Chen et al. (2018); Austin et al. (2021), code feature summarization Liu et al. (2021); Moreno et al. (2013); Scalabrino et al. (2017); Wan et al. (2018); Alon et al. (2018); Moreno et al. (2014), code search Husain et al. (2019); Feng et al. (2020), unit test generation Tufano et al. (2021) and even bug fixing Drain et al. (2021) and detection Zhai et al. (2020). This paper naturally is an extension and evaluation of personalized unit test generations as studied by Tufano et al. (2021), and an important contribution to the understanding optimization in a deployment scenario.

Much of the previous literature on personalized models focuses on client-side training to keep data on device Shor et al. (2019); Popov et al. (2018), and most work is in the domain of search query completion Jaech and Ostendorf (2018), natural language completion Popov et al. (2018), or even automated speech recognition Shor et al. (2019). Naturally this work extends the domain of evaluation beyond natural language tasks and into the software engineering domain. This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters. At the time of writing such large models cannot be executed in a reasonable amount of time on most consumer laptops. We leave to future work extending these studies to models which have been pruned, quantized, distilled, and optimized to be ran in limited resource environments.

## 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper we explored different ways to customize a code generation model for a given codebase, with the goal of improving its performances on a target project. We described and analyzed four customization strategies and applied them on 20 different software projects for the task of generating unit test cases. Specifically, we considered the following strategies: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned on the target project; (ii) _L-EO_ fine-tuning, a lightweight training which freezes most of the model’s parameters, tuning only embedding and output layers; (iii) _L-LDB_ fine-tuning, a lightweight training which only tunes the last decoder block; (iv) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific vector (prefix).

In our extensive empirical evaluation we found that all the customization strategies lead to significant model’s improvements on a target project, in terms of both intrinsic and task-specific metrics, with the custom models adapting to the coding style of the target project. While there is no clear winner among the customization strategies, each approach can provide specific benefits in particular deployment scenarios.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a timely and practically motivated investigation into personalized fine-tuning strategies for transformer-based code generation models, specifically targeting unit test generation for Java projects. The topic is well-aligned with emerging challenges in deploying large language models (LLMs) in real-world, domain-specific settings—particularly in software engineering, where code style, library usage, and private repositories create significant domain gaps between pre-training and deployment. The clarity of exposition in the introduction and setup is strong: the motivation around generalization, data privacy, and deployment efficiency is convincingly articulated, and the three-way comparison between full fine-tuning, lightweight tuning, and prefix tuning offers a structured framework for analyzing trade-offs between performance and cost. However, the manuscript, particularly in the early sections, lacks sufficient methodological detail and formal grounding to fully support its empirical claims. For instance, the description of "lightweight fine-tuning" is cut off mid-exposition (ending at "we achieve this"), undermining confidence in the completeness of the technical presentation. Additionally, while the problem of personalized adaptation is well-motivated, the paper would benefit from deeper engagement with related work in parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., adapter modules, LoRA) and personalization in NLP to position its contributions more precisely. The evaluation metrics are mentioned but not yet detailed in this excerpt, leaving unclear how "task-specific" performance is quantitatively assessed beyond standard NLP metrics like BLEU or perplexity, which are known to be weak proxies for functional correctness in code generation. Overall, the paper has strong potential due to its practical relevance and clear structure, but the current version requires completion of technical sections and more rigorous framing of its novelty and evaluation methodology to meet the standards of a top-tier conference like ICLR.

comments.title: Inconsistent and ambiguous taxonomy of lightweight fine-tuning approaches in abstract and early text
comments.quote: We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model’s parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific prefix vector.
comments.explanation: The abstract and initial text present 'lightweight fine-tuning' as a single method, while the paper later reveals it encompasses two distinct methods: L-EO and L-LDB. This oversimplification misrepresents the study's design—especially since Table 1 and Figure 1 list four methods, not three. Although Section 2.3 and 2.4 clarify that 'lightweight' is an umbrella category, this nuance is absent early on and risks misleading readers. A clearer framing would explicitly acknowledge two variants under 'lightweight' in the high-level description to maintain accuracy and avoid underrepresentation of the methodological scope.

comments.title: Incorrect and misleading justification for prefix tuning parameter count
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The formula includes unexplained factors of 24 and 2. The factor 24 likely refers to the 24 transformer layers (12 encoder + 12 decoder), implying a separate trainable prefix per layer—a non-standard variant not described in the paper. Standard prefix tuning uses shared prefix vectors or reparameterization without per-layer parameters. If layer-specific prefixes were used, this architectural choice should be clearly stated. The factor of 2 may refer to key/value transformations or encoder/decoder separation, but its role is ambiguous. While the final count (10M) matches Table 1 and is internally consistent, the derivation misrepresents the method and cannot be reproduced without clarification. This undermines confidence in the efficiency claims and constitutes a moderate issue due to underspecification.

comments.title: Spurious abbreviation 'Prefx' used in tables
comments.explanation: The abbreviation 'Prefx' appears in Table 1 and Table 3, clearly intended as 'Prefix'. While context resolves the ambiguity, the use of a truncated, non-standard label in formal academic tables reduces clarity and professionalism. Full, correctly spelled method names should be used in tables to ensure consistency and readability, especially for technical terms like 'Prefix tuning' that may be cited or referenced. This is a stylistic inconsistency rather than a logical or technical flaw, but it affects presentation quality.

comments.title: Flawed compute cost model underestimates actual training cost for lightweight methods
comments.quote: For a model with _N_ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be _C \approx_ 2 _N_ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers.
comments.explanation: The paper applies the _C \approx 2N_ FLOP rule correctly for forward pass estimation, but misapplies it to justify cost savings in lightweight tuning. It claims lower cost due to fewer trainable parameters, asserting backward pass scaling with only trainable parameters. However, in standard training, forward and backward passes still traverse the entire network to propagate gradients to trainable layers, unless activation checkpointing is used (not mentioned). Thus, FLOP cost per step remains close to full model cost. Only the optimizer update scales with trained parameters. This invalidates the per-step cost justification used in RQ3's efficiency comparisons. While convergence trends may still hold, the reported cost-performance trade-offs are misleadingly attributed to FLOP reductions. This is a major issue as it affects the validity of a core research claim.

comments.title: Imperfect reproducibility of task-specific evaluation metrics
comments.quote: - _Perfect Matches_ : We compare the model’s output string with the target developer-written unit test. If the two strings are identical, this is considered a perfect match. We do not take spacing and indentation into account... - _Abstracted Code Matches_ : We pass the model output and target output through the src2abs tool (Tufano, 2018), to obtain an abstracted version, masking variable names, method names, etc.
comments.explanation: While task-specific metrics are valuable, key implementation details are missing. For 'perfect match', the handling of whitespace, line breaks, access modifiers, and syntax elements is not fully specified. For 'abstracted code match', the abstraction level of src2abs is underspecified—e.g., how identifiers, literals, types, or control structures are treated. Without a precise description or example, reproduction is difficult. Given the central role of these metrics in evaluating model effectiveness (RQ2), and the paper’s emphasis on large-scale experiments (320 models, 20 projects), clearer definitions or illustrative examples should be provided to ensure transparency and reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of lightweight fine-tuning approaches in abstract vs. main text
comments.quote: We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) _custom_ fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model’s parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone; (iii) _prefix_ tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific prefix vector.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the abstract presents only three customization strategies, but the main text and figures clearly describe four approaches: custom, L-EO, L-LDB, and prefix tuning. This initially raised a concern about misrepresentation or omission. However, upon reading Section 2.3 and 2.4, it becomes clear that the term 'lightweight fine-tuning' is used as a category encompassing two specific variants: L-EO and L-LDB. The abstract uses 'lightweight fine-tuning' as a single bullet point, implying a single method, while in reality it hides two distinct approaches. Although the authors later clarify this, the abstract oversimplifies the taxonomy and could mislead a reader into thinking only three total methods were evaluated (especially since Table 1 and Figure 1 list four). This risks underrepresenting the study’s design. A clearer phrasing would explicitly call out two lightweight variants under the umbrella.

comments.title: Ambiguity in parameter count calculation for prefix tuning
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 × 200 × 24 × 2 ≈ 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: The formula 1024 × 200 × 24 × 2 appears suspicious: the factor of 24 is not defined anywhere. Given that the model is BART (12 encoder and 12 decoder layers), and prefix tuning appends a prefix to each transformer block as per Figure 1d, it's plausible that 24 refers to the total number of transformer blocks (12 enc + 12 dec). This aligns with the description in Liu et al. (2021) where prefix vectors are maintained per layer. Assuming prefix vectors of size 200 are added at each of the 24 layers (each of dimension 1024), trainable parameters are 200 × 1024 × 24 = ~4.9M. But the factor of 2 in 2 × 24 × 200 × 1024 suggests a possible double-counting—perhaps misunderstanding whether the prefix is replicated per layer or learned independently. However, the final result (10M) is close to 2 × 4.9M = 9.8M, raising concern whether the formula incorrectly doubles a correct quantity. But given Table 1 lists 10M parameters for prefix tuning (2.4% of 416M), and no other explanation fits, it's likely the formula meant (prefix_length) × (hidden_size) × (num_layers × 2 paths?): if prefixes are added to encoder and decoder inputs separately per layer, and each has full embedding dimension, the count could be 200 × 1024 × 24. The ×2 may be a OCR or typo for ×1. But since the stated 10M matches the table, the calculation may just be poorly explained rather than wrong. Still, the lack of definition for '24' and extra ×2 is ambiguous.

comments.title: Mismatch between described lightweight fine-tuning scope and L-LDB notation
comments.quote: (ii) _lightweight_ fine-tuning, which only optimizes the token embedding representations or the final softmax layer;
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction describe 'lightweight fine-tuning' as involving only the embeddings or the final softmax layer, which corresponds to L-EO. But the paper also introduces L-LDB (tuning only the last decoder block) as another form of lightweight tuning. This creates inconsistency: the high-level description only mentions embedding/output tuning, omitting the architectural layer-wise tuning strategy. However, since the term 'lightweight fine-tuning' is later used as a category (Section 2.3 and 2.4), and two sub-approaches are explicitly defined (L-EO and L-LDB), the concern is resolved with deeper reading. The issue is not an error per se, but a potential ambiguity in how 'lightweight' is scoped early on. A less careful reader might assume only embedding/output tuning counts as lightweight, which would misrepresent the study. A minor framing concern due to early imprecise categorization.

comments.title: Incorrect implication of BLEU improvement in abstract
comments.quote: BLEU going from 16.1 achieved by the baseline model to 36-38 by custom models.
comments.explanation: This statement from Section 4.1 appears to be a summary of results not in the provided passage but in the context. The quoted text is from the results section and does not appear in the passage to check. Therefore, no issue is raised in the actual passage provided. (Note: This was initially flagged when scanning for claims about BLEU, but upon verifying the passage boundaries, it’s not part of the section being checked.)

comments.title: Incorrect Calculation of Prefix Tuning Parameters
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the formula appears to compute the number of prefix parameters. The prefix is described as a sequence of 200 virtual tokens with an embedding size of 1024. In prefix tuning, each such token corresponds to a learned vector of size d_model (here 1024), so we would expect 200 × 1024 = 204,800 parameters per prefix (encoder or decoder). Li and Liang (2021) originally proposed prefix vectors added to both encoder and decoder inputs, so if both are tunable, we would expect 2 × 200 × 1024 ≈ 0.41M parameters. However, the paper states \(1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2\), which includes a mysterious factor of 24 — possibly the number of layers (BART-large has 12 encoder and 12 decoder layers, totaling 24). But in standard prefix tuning, the same prefix vector is not replicated per layer; instead, a single prefix is injected and transformations are handled via the self-attention mechanism. Some variants (e.g., 'reparameterized prefix tuning') do involve layer-specific transformations, but the paper does not indicate such a modification. Given that Table 1 reports 10M trainable parameters (2.4% of 406M), and 1024 × 200 × 24 × 2 = 9,830,400 ≈ 10M, the number matches the calculation, but the justification is misleading or incorrect. The formula implies a non-standard, layer-wise prefix mechanism without clarifying this modification. This is a real issue: unless the model architecture differs significantly from standard prefix tuning, the parameter count is based on a misdescription of the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation: L-EO vs L-LDB Parameter Counts in Text vs Table
comments.quote: During L-EO finetuining, instead, only 13% (53M) parameters are trained. The L-LDB fientuning reduces the number of trainable parameters to 4.2% (17M).
comments.explanation: The numbers cited (53M and 17M) match Table 1, which is correct. However, the term 'finetuining' in 'L-EO finetuining' appears to be a typo due to OCR (likely intended as 'fine-tuning'), and 'fientuning' similarly for L-LDB. This would normally not be flagged per instructions, but the inconsistency across similar phrases (e.g., 'fientuning' vs 'finetuining') raises concern about OCR corruption. However, since both occurrences are clearly OCR artifacts ('fientuning' → 'fine-tuning') and the correct values (4.2%, 17M) align with Table 1 and context, no real error remains. The core numerical consistency is preserved, so this resolves upon OCR correction. No action is needed.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Figure 1c Caption and Method Name
comments.quote: (c) L-LDB allows to train only the parameters of the last decoder block
comments.explanation: In the caption of Figure 1, 'L-LDB' is used to denote training only the last decoder block. However, in Section 2.4, the acronym L-LDB is defined as 'Lightweight fine-tuning - Last Decoder Block', which is consistent. The acronym is used uniformly across the figure and text. While 'L-LDB' might be slightly nonstandard or redundant (since 'L' already stands for 'Lightweight'), it is self-consistent. No actual inconsistency exists — the notation is introduced and maintained properly. The authors clearly define L-LDB and use it consistently.

comments.title: Ambiguous Use of 'Total Parameters' in Table 1 for Prefix Tuning
comments.explanation: The row for 'Prefx' (likely OCR for 'Prefix') lists total parameters as 416M, an increase from 406M due to the 10M added prefix parameters. This is accurate in principle. However, the percentage of trainable parameters is reported as 2.4% of the total (10M / 416M ≈ 2.4%), which is technically correct. The potential ambiguity is whether '2.4%' is relative to 406M or 416M. But since 10M / 406M ≈ 2.46%, it's nearly the same. More importantly, the paper states: 'only 2.4% (10M) of the total', and 'total' clearly refers to 416M here. This is consistent and transparent. However, a reader might momentarily think the percentage is relative to the base model, but the context resolves this. The clarity could be improved, but it doesn't rise to a real issue.

comments.title: Spurious Factor of 24 in Prefix Tuning Parameter Calculation
comments.quote: 1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10M
comments.explanation: As in the earlier issue, the factor of 24 is unexplained. If this refers to the 24 transformer layers (12 encoder + 12 decoder), it would suggest that a separate prefix vector is learned per layer — which contradicts standard prefix tuning, where a single sequence of prefixes is prepended to the input and their influence is propagated via attention. Some follow-up works (e.g., 'Deep Prefix Tuning') do explore layer-specific prefixes, but the paper does not cite or describe such a variant. The use of '24' without justification makes the method underspecified. A reader cannot reproduce the setup unless they assume a non-standard architecture. The result (10M) matches Table 1, so the number isn't wrong, but the justification is either incorrect or refers to an unexplained architectural choice. This is not a mathematical error per se, but it reflects an underspecified method with potentially misleading derivation.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'Prefx' in Table 1 and Table 3
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that 'Prefx' was a typographical error for 'Prefix', potentially indicating a lack of attention to detail or an inconsistency in notation that could confuse readers. Given that the rest of the paper consistently uses 'Prefix tuning' as a term (e.g., in Section 2.5 and Figure 1), and the OCR context note explicitly warns about potential misreads, I considered whether this might be an OCR artifact—such as a missing character ('i') due to scanning error. However, the same abbreviation 'Prefx' appears again in Table 3 in the header row (`|Project BLEU4 Perplexity Base Cust. L-EO L-LDB Prefx|`), suggesting it is intentionally used in the tables, not just a one-off OCR glitch. While context makes the intended meaning clear—there is no other customization method it could refer to—the use of an abbreviated, non-standard form in formal tables undermines clarity and consistency. Standard academic practice would preserve full, correctly spelled labels in tables for precision. This is especially important given that 'Prefix' is a technical term with a specific definition in the literature (Li & Liang, 2021).

comments.title: Discrepancy in Prefix Tuning Parameter Count Justification
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the formula $1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2$ seemed unjustifiably large. Breaking it down: embedding size (1024) × prefix length (200) suggests 204,800 parameters per prefix block. But the multiplication by 24 × 2 is puzzling. The paper states the prefix is added to the input of *every* encoder and decoder block. BART has 12 encoder and 12 decoder layers, totaling 24 blocks. Thus, if a separate prefix is trained per block, we'd have 24 × 1024 × 200 = ~49.1M parameters—far more than 10M. The factor of 2 suggests perhaps separate key and value projections for prefix tuning (a known variant), but even then: 24 blocks × 2 (key/value) × 1024 × 200 ≈ 98.3M. The approximation to 10M suggests the actual count is about 4.8M, which aligns with *shared* prefix embeddings across blocks and only key/value transformations—common in prefix tuning implementations. Yet the formula provided does not reflect this. The paper's claim of 10M trainable parameters *is* consistent with Table 1 and the 2.4% figure (10M/416M), so the *value* is plausible. However, the derivation misleads: either the 24 is wrong (layers are shared), or the 2 is not needed, or both. The explanation lacks clarity on architectural choices (shared vs. independent prefixes), making it difficult for readers to reproduce the method. Context resolves the final number, but not the correctness of the formula.

comments.title: Ambiguity in L-LDB Trained Parameter Count vs. Definition
comments.quote: The L-LDB fientuning reduces the number of trainable parameters to 4.2% (17M).
comments.explanation: I noticed that L-LDB is defined as training only the last decoder block—comprising self-attention, encoder-decoder attention, layernorm, and feedforward layers. For a BART-large model (12 layers, hidden size 1024, ffn size 4096), the last decoder block should contain roughly: two layer norms (~2×1024), one self-attention (3×1024×1024 + 1024), one cross-attention (3×1024×1024 + 1024), and one FFN (2×1024×4096 + 4096), totaling approximately 16-18M parameters—consistent with the 17M cited. So the number appears correct. However, the definition says 'encoder-decoder attention' is trained, which is present in each decoder layer, so this is properly included. The concern arises only because the formula or precise derivation isn't given, but the value matches expectations based on standard BART architecture. While no actual error remains, the paper could have been clearer by referencing model architecture assumptions (e.g., hidden size, ffn dimension) to support reproducibility. As it stands, a reader must trust the number or reverse-engineer it from context.

comments.title: Incorrect Reference to Figure 3 Without Visual
comments.quote: The results in terms of task-specific performances are presented in Figure 3. The plot 3a shows the top-k accuracy for perfect matches (solid line) and abstracted matches (dotted line), aggregated over the 20 projects.
comments.explanation: As a reviewer, I initially tried to verify whether the stated trends (Custom > Prefix ≈ L-LDB > L-EO) were supported by the data in Figure 3. However, Figure 3 is not included in the provided context. The text refers to plots 3a and 3b with specific details (e.g., solid vs. dotted lines, distribution of cosine similarities), which suggests the figure exists and is informative. But without it, the reader cannot assess the strength of the claims, such as the near-identical performance of Custom, Prefix, and L-LDB on abstracted matches or the skew of cosine similarity distributions. That said, the authors are not at fault for the missing figure in this OCR-extracted context—it was likely present in the original PDF. Therefore, while the *current extraction* makes validation impossible, this is not an authorial flaw. No issue is flagged under the given instructions, which state not to flag missing references if they exist elsewhere.

comments.title: Prefix Tuning Parameter Count Calculation Appears Incorrect
comments.quote: Practically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 \times 200 \times 24 \times 2 \approx 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this formula seems to imply that the 24 and 2 are multipliers arising from model architecture. However, based on standard prefix tuning implementations and the paper's own definitions, the trainable prefix is a set of continuous vectors added to each transformer block's input. The prefix length is 200, embedding size is 1024, and if prefixes are added to both encoder and decoder blocks, the number of parameters should be: 200 (length) \times 1024 (embed dim) \times 2 (encoder + decoder) = 409,600 \times 2 = 819,200, which is ~0.82M, not 10M. The formula multiplies by 24, which likely corresponds to the number of transformer blocks (12 encoder + 12 decoder), but in prefix tuning, the same prefix vectors are typically shared across all blocks or replicated without per-block parameters being independently trainable. The authors may intend that a unique prefix is learned for each of the 24 blocks (12 encoder + 12 decoder), leading to 200 \times 1024 \times 24 \times 2? But that would double count (\times 2) since 24 already includes both encoder and decoder). The correct count, if prefixes are learned per block, should be 200 \times 1024 \times 24 = 4,915,200 (~4.9M), still not 10M. Alternatively, if it's 200 \times 1024 \times 2 (only one prefix per encoder stack and one per decoder stack), it's ~0.82M. The paper later states prefix tuning has 10M trainable parameters (~2.4% of 416M), and Table 1 lists 10M. To reach ~10M, possible explanations include: (1) OCR error: \times 24 might be \times 12 (if 12 blocks per side and prefixes per block), 200 \times 1024 \times 12 \times 2 = 4.91M; still not 10M; (2) the \times 24 might be a mistake and should be \times 24 \times 2 if they mean 24 blocks and 2 vector components (unlikely); or (3) they include additional parameters like prefix-output mapping. But no such detail is mentioned. The most plausible scenario consistent with ~10M is 200 (length) \times 1024 (dim) \times 24 (blocks) \times 2 (input + output per block or some other duplication), but this contradicts conventional prefix tuning. Given that 10M appears in Table 1 and is treated as consistent, the derivation in the main text is likely incorrect or poorly explained. This matters because it undermines confidence in the reported efficiency claim of prefix tuning having only 10M trainable parameters — the justification is flawed.

comments.title: L-LDB Trainable Parameter Count Inconsistency with Later Claim
comments.quote: The L-LDB fientuning reduces the number of trainable parameters to 4.2% (17M).
comments.explanation: In Table 1 and the text, L-LDB is said to have 17M trainable parameters (4.2% of 406M). Later, in the 'Key Claims' section of the context, it says 'Custom, Prefix, and L-LDB perform comparably on abstracted matches; L-EO performs slightly worse.' This seems consistent. However, in the same summary, it states: 'Custom, L-EO, L-LDB, and prefix tuning result in 100%, 13%, 4.2%, and 2.4% of parameters trained, respectively.' This matches. But in Section 2.4, the claim that parameter changes are highest in the last decoder block is used to justify L-LDB. A deeper concern is whether 17M is reasonable for just the last decoder block. A BART-large model has 12 decoder layers. The last decoder block would include self-attention (Q,K,V projections: 3 \times 1024 \times 1024), encoder-decoder attention (another 3 \times 1024 \times 1024), feedforward layer (~4 \times 1024^2), and layernorms (~2 \times 1024). Total per layer is roughly 3\times + 3\times + 4\times = 10\times 1024^2 ≈ 10.5M per layer. Even one decoder layer would exceed 10M, so 17M for one block is plausible (if including some shared parameters). So 17M seems reasonable. The text says 'last decoder block' — does 'block' mean one layer or all decoder layers? Given that encoder-decoder attention is mentioned (which only exists in decoder layers), and that there are 12 decoder layers, training only the 'last block' likely means the final decoder *layer*. The parameter count (17M) fits. So while the word 'block' might be ambiguous, context resolves it as one layer. No error here.

comments.title: Compute Cost Formula Lacks Justification and May Be Misapplied
comments.quote: For a model with _N_ parameters, we approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be _C ≈_ 2 _N_ floating point operations per training token, with an additional correction for embedding layers.
comments.explanation: The rule of thumb _C ≈ 2N_ FLOPs per token for a forward pass is widely cited in scaling literature (e.g., Kaplan et al., 2020) and originates from the observation that each parameter is involved in roughly two FLOPs (multiply-add). This is standard and correctly referenced. However, the paper applies this formula to compute cost comparisons across methods with different numbers of trainable parameters, but the same total model size. The issue arises in how backward pass cost is described: 'backward pass costing ~2× forward pass but only applied to trainable parameters'. This is misleading. In practice, gradients must be computed for all layers in the computational graph up to the trainable ones. While frozen layers do not update weights, their forward and backward passes still occur during training, unless activation checkpointing or other optimizations are used. If only the last decoder block is trainable (L-LDB), the activations of earlier layers must still be computed and stored (or recomputed) for gradient flow — so the forward pass cost is still ~2N for the full model, not just the trainable portion. The backward pass FLOPs for non-trainable layers may be skipped only if their gradients are not needed — but they are, since they feed into trainable layers. Thus, only the weight update step (e.g., optimizer step) is reduced for fewer parameters. The dominant FLOP cost — forward and backward pass through the full network — remains close to full model cost, not scaled by trainable parameter count. Therefore, the paper's compute cost approximation likely underestimates the cost of lightweight methods. For instance, it may claim L-LDB is cheaper because only 17M params are trained, but in reality, the FLOP cost per token is nearly the same as full fine-tuning. The claim that 'Custom models achieve fastest convergence and best performance at low compute budgets; L-LDB catches up at higher compute' may still hold due to better signal or dynamics, but the justification based on per-step FLOPs is unsound. This affects the validity of RQ3, which compares cost-performance trade-offs. The paper cites Kaplan et al. (2020), which discusses FLOPs for training but assumes full updates. No mention is made of gradient checkpointing or selective backprop, so the standard interpretation applies. This is a significant flaw in the cost model.

comments.title: Perfect and Abstracted Match Metrics Lack Reproducibility Details
comments.quote: - _Perfect Matches_ : We compare the model’s output string with the target developer-written unit test. If the two strings are identical, this is considered a perfect match. We do not take spacing and indentation into account... - _Abstracted Code Matches_ : We pass the model output and target output through the src2abs tool (Tufano, 2018), to obtain an abstracted version, masking variable names, method names, etc.
comments.explanation: The authors rightly use task-specific metrics beyond BLEU, which is important. However, key details are missing that would prevent reproduction. For 'perfect match', ignoring spacing and indentation is stated, but it's unclear what normalization is applied. Are line breaks ignored? Are multiple spaces collapsed? Are keywords like 'public' vs 'private' abstracted? For Java, access modifiers may matter. More critically, for 'abstracted code matches', the src2abs tool is referenced but not described. The level of abstraction — e.g., are all identifiers replaced with 'ID', or typed IDs? Are literals abstracted? Are class names masked? The paper says 'masking variable names, method names, etc.', but 'etc.' is underspecified. Without a precise definition or public tool, it's impossible to know whether 'abstracted match' means syntactic equality after identifier replacement, or includes deeper normalization like control flow abstraction. This is crucial because the claim that custom models achieve ~13–14% abstracted match vs ~3% baseline depends entirely on how abstraction is done. If abstraction is too coarse, differences may be erased; if too fine, it may not help generalization. The measure is central to RQ2, yet underspecified. While the tool is cited, a high-level description or example would have helped. Given that the paper emphasizes reproducibility (320 models, 20 projects), these metrics should be more fully specified.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The manuscript provides a well-motivated exploration of personalized model fine-tuning for code generation, addressing the critical tension between model performance and the logistical costs of deploying specialized models in enterprise environments. The clarity of the problem statement is a significant strength; by characterizing customization through three distinct lenses—full fine-tuning, lightweight adjustment, and prefix tuning—the authors position their work to provide actionable, nuanced deployment trade-offs. However, the current draft faces a potential issue with the novelty and depth of the methodological contribution. While the application to Java unit test generation is practical, the described techniques (specifically prefix-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning) are established in the broader NLP literature. To elevate the paper, the authors must ensure that the empirical evaluation offers deep, non-obvious insights into how code-specific syntax and long-range structural dependencies influence these specific adaptation strategies, rather than simply confirming that fine-tuning improves performance. Furthermore, the abstract and introduction provide a clear roadmap, but the transition from general definitions to technical implementation in Section 2 needs to move quickly beyond high-level descriptions to provide the rigorous mathematical or architectural rigor required for the ICLR level.

comments.title: Inconsistency in number and definition of fine-tuning strategies
comments.quote: We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) custom fine-tuning... (ii) lightweight fine-tuning... (iii) prefix tuning...
comments.explanation: The paper promises three fine-tuning strategies but defines four (Custom, Prefix, and two distinct 'lightweight' versions: L-EO and L-LDB). Furthermore, the definition of 'lightweight' in the abstract is underspecified, creating confusion regarding whether it refers to a category or a single technique. The text should reconcile the high-level summary with the experimental design.

comments.title: Mismatched model architecture assumptions and parameter counts
comments.quote: Specifically, we set the prefix length to 200 tokens, and thus with an embedding size of 1024, this gives a total of 1024 × 200 × 24 × 2 ≈ 10M trainable parameters.
comments.explanation: Calculations for parameter counts are inconsistent. If using a 406M parameter model (BART-Large, 24 layers), the arithmetic equals ~9.8M, but if using BART-Base (12 layers), the count should be ~5M. The paper conflates the depth of BART-Large with the description of a standard BART architecture without confirming the specific transformer configuration used, leading to an ambiguous parameter accounting.

comments.title: Inconsistency in data reporting and calculation errors
comments.explanation: The reported average BLEU score for 'Cust.' (38.4) in Table 3 contradicts the qualitative range stated in the text (36-38) and appears unsupported by the manual arithmetic mean of the column (~36.7). These discrepancies undermine the reliability of the reported aggregate performance gains.

comments.title: Methodological errors in statistical testing
comments.quote: Kruskal-Wallis Test p-values testing the significance of the pairwise hypothesis
comments.explanation: The paper cites Table 4 for statistical significance, but the table is missing. Furthermore, the authors incorrectly use the Kruskal-Wallis test (an omnibus test) to interpret pairwise significance without appropriate post-hoc tests (e.g., Dunn’s test). This renders the claim of p < 1e-7 statistically unverifiable and theoretically flawed.

comments.title: Inaccurate approximation of transformer compute costs
comments.quote: We approximate the computation cost of a forward pass to be C ≈ 2 N floating point operations per training token
comments.explanation: The estimate of 2N FLOPs per token for a forward pass is a significant underestimate compared to the standard convention of ~6N FLOPs for Transformer architectures. Using this value to define the X-axis of performance-cost graphs misrepresents the actual computational intensity of the methods.

comments.title: Weak justification for layer-wise fine-tuning heuristic
comments.quote: We hypothesize that it could be sufficient to tune the last decoder block
comments.explanation: The authors justify tuning only the last decoder block based on observation of weight changes rather than sensitivity analysis or formal ablation. While acceptable for early-stage empirical work, the logical leap from 'last blocks change the most' to 'only last blocks are necessary' lacks theoretical grounding.
